source_file: [Day 1] Adventure Kit; 30 Days Lost in Space (English_ASR).srt
full_text: Oh my that's definitely not good I'm seeing a lot of red on this error log a shame you can't just restore from a backup like myself I'm sure even you could guess your life is in a rather precarious situation and there's no time to waste so let's get started shall we allow me to first introduce the foundational material that is so crucial to ensuring your longevity the Inventr.io HERO microcontroller strange what they considered micro back then in simplistic terms that your biological brain can compute it's like a tiny simplified computer that we can program to do our bidding and given our dire circumstances is about to become your best friend well second best friend also Al included in your repair kit is a programming computer it has been modified to contain a complete holographic copy of the old internet thankfully while it does seem to be more powerful than the HERO board it does not have the connectivity to control your ship systems as such its ability is limited to writing the code that the HERO board will execute another vital component we're going to use is an integrated development environment or IDE for short you will use this software to write a small program or sketch and upload code to the HERO board we will dive into how to achieve that a bit later but for now please install the Arduino IDE software onto your computer it is my understanding that this is a lot to digest and maybe puzzling at times but please do not be concerned as I am well-versed in the use of such technology and am readily prepared to guide you through each essential step of the process our mission today is to assess the condition of the repair kit identify the operating system of the programming computer and start the process of getting the HERO board set up and integrated into your craft systems if it's not already abundantly clear time is ticking and the state of your existence is on the line I propose we get to it all right Explorer it's time to roll up your sleeves and prepare to tackle the first task at hand to begin you'll find a USB cable included in the kit another quaint artifact from the 21st century we can use this cable that we are lucky enough to have to connect the hero to the programming computer this little wire will be the lifeblood of our operation powering the hero and also allowing us to transfer code to it one end of that cable should fit into the HERO board and the other will fit into your USB port on your programming computer make that that connection first need I remind you of rule number one of your employee training manual when you're in space or in this case water always make sure you're Tethered to something we'll work by creating smaller circuits that will stimulate your ship systems while we create the code for each pair then we can plug the ship systems into the hero as each program is completed once you have the HERO board connected we're going to need to communicate with it that's where the Arduino IDE comes in start by loading up one of the example sketches included with the Arduino IDE select the file menu examples 01 Basics and finally blink this will open up the blink sketch a pre-written piece of code that will blink a light emitting diode or LED for short on your HERO board this shows that the HERO board is working correctly when you start a new sketch or load an existing sketch you first need to configure the proper board and indicate which port Port is connected to your board go to the tools menu then select board and make sure Arduino Uno is selected since your HERO board is compatible with that model next you'll need to select the correct Port again go to the tools menu and this time select Port you should see a few options select the one that refers to the Arduino Uno if you're unsure of which one to choose just disconnect the board and see which one disappears then reconnect and select that Port speaking of LEDs your heroboard has a number of LEDs mounted on its surface one of which we can control LEDs are great for debugging purposes or in our case to ensure everything is set up correctly all right Explorer now that we're all set up it's time to get into gear and bring that led on the HERO board to life we're going to compile and upload the blink sketch that you've already loaded in the IDE you will see two round buttons with arrows in in the top left corner of the window the first one with the check mark checks for any spelling or formatting mistakes also known as syntax errors and compiles if it passes click on that button to compile your sketch my Diagnostics show that your code should be error free which means you're ready to upload it to the HERO board great job Explorer the second round button with the right arrow is for uploading the code click that button and the Arduino IDE will start uploading your sketch to the HERO board if everything is in order as it should be the IDE will display a message that says done uploading and this our HERO board's LED is now blinking on and off every second how spectacular if you've followed all my instructions precisely then you've just successfully completed the first part of your repair Journey you're one blink closer to seeing the inside of your mother ship again now that you've got the board successfully connect and run some rudimentary code let's briefly go over what just happened in a bit more detail by taking a look at the code all right Explorer we've uploaded our blink.ino sketch and now we're going to decode it much like how you might decipher alien glyphs you'll notice grayed out text that doesn't appear to affect the sketch those are comments they're like the sticky notes you leave yourself to remember why you decided to place a carbon dioxide scrubber next to the coffee machine at the top of the blink sketch is an example of a multi-line comment that begins with slash-asterisk and ends with asterisk slash even if those characters are on another line anything between slash asterisk and asterisk slash is considered a comment and will be ignored when compiling or building the sketch you could pin your magnum opus between these markers and the IDE would blissfully disregard it single line comments start with a double slash and are used to add helpful notes or to temporarily disable a part of the code anything following double slash up to the end of the same line is also ignored by the compiler next we have setup and loop the synergistic pair of Arduino programming you may think of them as the heart and lungs of your sketch each equally essential with its own crucial role to play the setup function sets up the variable definitions and initial tasks it's a function that's run once and only once when your program first starts this is where you put all your preparations initializations pinMode declarations and opening Communications so many as for instance if we're using an LED we need to tell the HERO board that we've connected an LED to a particular pin and that it should be an output pin this would go in the setup to assist you in better understanding please refer to this example now Loop is where as you'd say the magic happens after setup finishes the program jumps to Loop and as you might guess this function Loops over and over again for as long as your board is powered repeatedly forever so if you want to Blink an LED turning it on and off would happen inside the loop like this that's the basic purpose of the setup and loop functions the former sets the stage and the latter keeps the show running all clear Explorer to explain the other functions digitalWrite is your digital switch it can set a digital pin on your HERO board to a specific State either low Zer volts or High 5 volts think of it as an onoff switch the Syntax for the digitalWrite function is straightforward digitalWrite parenthesis pin comma value n parenthesis pin is the number of the pin you want to control and value is either high or low so if you want to turn the LED connected to pin 12 on you'd write digitalWrite parenthesis 12 comma high end parenthesis to turn it off you'd write digitalWrite parenthesis 12 comma low end parenthesis you might notice that in that example blink sketch it doesn't use a number for the pin we'll discuss a bit more about that tomorrow but for today just know that when you configure a board in the Arduino IDE there are some names defined for you in this case LED_BUILTIN is defined in the Arduino id13 which is the pin that the built-in LED is controlled by next the delay function does just what an observant Explorer would expect it delays execution before continuing on to the next statement the delay is for a specified period of milliseconds which is 1,000th of a second if you want the program to do nothing for a second you'd write delay parenthesis 1,000 n parenthesis semicolon since 1,000 milliseconds equals 1 second in the context of blinking an LED digitalWrite is used to turn the LED on or off and delay is used to control the length of time the LED stays on or off together they create the perfect blinking effect aha starting to see the light are we well at least half the time for your benefit let's turn to some instructional material that I was miraculously able to gather cached on the computer I've also added this to our mission resources for future reference you're welcome well well Explorer you've managed to make a tiny light blink how terribly impressive the interstellar Community is no doubt holding its breath in awe but please let's not forget the Minor Detail that you are sitting in abysmal Darkness 60 M under an alien sea while your rapidly dwindling oxygen supply bubbles away it's imperative that we restore that battery charging system in order to extend your oxygen supply and buy us some time rest assured every passing second I'm not working on saving your life I'll be processing how we might avoid a similar fate the next time your curiosity gets the better of you no pressure it's only your invaluable life that is at stake so tuck yourself in dear Explorer dream of blinking LEDs and getting out of this mess you got yourself in we'll need your brain fully operational tomorrow if we're to start getting your ship systems back under control please do try not to attract any attention as I've detected some rather peculiar local sea life nearby sweet dreams

up to the end of the same line is also ignored by the compiler next we have setup and loop the synergistic pair of Arduino programming you may think of them as the heart and lungs of your sketch each equally essential with its own crucial role to play the setup function sets up the variable definitions and initial tasks it's a function that's run once and only once when your program first starts this is where you put all your preparations initializations pinMode declarations and opening Communications so many as for instance if we're using an LED we need to tell the HERO board that we've connected an LED to a particular pin and that it should be an output pin this would go in the setup to assist you in better understanding please refer to this example now Loop is where as you'd say the magic happens after setup finishes the program jumps to Loop and as you might guess this function Loops over and over again for as long as your board is powered repeatedly forever so if you want to Blink an LED turning it on and off would happen inside the loop like this that's the basic purpose of the setup and loop functions the former sets the stage and the latter keeps the show running all clear Explorer to explain the other functions digitalWrite is your digital switch it can set a digital pin on your HERO board to a specific State either low Zer volts or High 5 volts think of it as an onoff switch the Syntax for the digitalWrite function is straightforward digitalWrite parenthesis pin comma value n parenthesis pin is the number of the pin you want to control and value is either high or low so if you want to turn the LED connected to pin 12 on you'd write digitalWrite parenthesis 12 comma high end parenthesis to turn it off you'd write digitalWrite parenthesis 12 comma low end parenthesis you might notice that in that example blink sketch it doesn't use a number for the pin we'll discuss a bit more about that tomorrow but for today just know that when you configure a board in the Arduino IDE there are some names defined for you in this case LED_BUILTIN is defined in the Arduino id13 which is the pin that the built-in LED is controlled by next the delay function does just what an observant Explorer would expect it delays execution before continuing on to the next statement the delay is for a specified period of milliseconds which is 1,000th of a second if you want the program to do nothing for a second you'd write delay parenthesis 1,000 n parenthesis semicolon since 1,000 milliseconds equals 1 second in the context of blinking an LED digitalWrite is used to turn the LED on or off and delay is used to control the length of time the LED stays on or off together they create the perfect blinking effect aha starting to see the light are we well at least half the time for your benefit let's turn to some instructional material that I was miraculously able to gather cached on the computer I've also added this to our mission resources for future reference you're welcome well well Explorer you've managed to make a tiny light blink how terribly impressive the interstellar Community is no doubt holding its breath in awe but please let's not forget the Minor Detail that you are sitting in abysmal Darkness 60 M under an alien sea while your rapidly dwindling oxygen supply bubbles away it's imperative that we restore that battery charging system in order to extend your oxygen supply and buy us some time rest assured every passing second I'm not working on saving your life I'll be processing how we might avoid a similar fate the next time your curiosity gets the better of you no pressure it's only your invaluable life that is at stake so tuck yourself in dear Explorer dream of blinking LEDs and getting out of this mess you got yourself in we'll need your brain fully operational tomorrow if we're to start getting your ship systems back under control please do try not to attract any attention as I've detected some rather peculiar local sea life nearby sweet dreams
